scenario_title: delete_rrset followed by add_rr for the same name/type
test_rationale: This test uses one UPDATE containing a delete_rrset followed immediately by an add_rr for the same owner/type. That shape directly exercises the RFC 2136 underspecification about intra-message evaluation order: a server may apply the delete first and then accept the new RR, yielding a final A RRset with the replacement address, or it may evaluate the add against the original RRset state or reduce the message to a net-effect conflict, producing a different reply and/or no observable post-state change. The follow-up QUERY makes the final zone state observable as a binary oracle.

group_1.behavior: Applies the delete_rrset then the add_rr incrementally, resulting in a final A RRset containing 192.0.2.20 and a successful update reply.

group_2.behavior: Treats the same-message delete_rrset plus add_rr as non-composable or evaluates the add against the original state, resulting in a different reply and/or no final A RRset.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §3.4.2.4 defines delete-rrset semantics, but RFC 2136 does not specify whether multiple prerequisite/operation records in a single UPDATE are processed incrementally against a working image or validated against the original zone state before any changes take effect. RFC 2181 §5.2 addresses RRset consistency/TTL, not intra-message ordering of delete-then-add for the same owner/type, so implementations may differ on whether the add sees the post-delete state or is rejected/collapsed.
